title: Apportionment methods in resource allocation
summary_en: Apportionment methods — rules for allocating indivisible resources (seats, quotas, budgets) proportionally to claims — are a classical topic in political-economy theory with deep practical applications. This paper applies apportionment methods to resource-allocation problems and compares their properties. The authors extend classical results to settings that go beyond political districting, showing which methods preserve proportionality under specific structural requirements. A rigorous contribution combining apportionment theory with operations-research practice.
data_used: Theoretical paper with algorithmic analysis; illustrative resource-allocation applications.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian public-resource-allocation design — e.g., education quota allocation, healthcare capacity distribution, regional development-fund splits, and voting-districting — apportionment methods provide a principled framework. Relevant to Oktatási Hivatal (university admissions), NEAK (hospital capacity), and Ministry of Economy (regional-fund allocation). Caveat: specific implementation requires fit with institutional constraints and political acceptability.
